item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What distance is that?

My thought was that it varies between people, hence "close enough" being the best approximation. I didn't think this minutiae point needed an exact measurement, but I don't mind tossing in a number if enough readers want to see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any human who observes these projections from a close enough distance

What distance is that? It seems odd for them Foundation to not mention it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Looks good to me! Thanks for the notice.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

This article has a lot of similarities with SCP-667, as was stated upthread. Both are plants that summon supernatural creatures, in the case of 667 faeries. Its not unlikely that the SCPF would look into whether both are correlated in some way. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

After two weeks, when the affected individual is next able to sleep, instances (usually 3 to 7 individuals) of SCP-1443-1 will materialize over the head of the sleeping subject. These projections are first seen originating from the forehead of the sleeping human; no changes in sleep mannerism have been recorded during this process. As a group, the SCP-1443-1 seek out a suitable location for rest, usually a patch of open soil. Should the SCP-1443-1 find a place of rest within fifteen minutes, they will cluster together and form a new individual of SCP-1443. If no such place is available, the SCP-1443-1 disappear when fifteen minutes have elapsed. Juvenile instances of SCP-1443 “planted” in this manner do not appear immediately capable of generating SCP-1443-1. The length of time required for these new plants to fully mature is currently unknown.

To this:

After two weeks, when the affected individual is next able to sleep, instances (usually 3 to 7 individuals) of SCP-1443-1 will materialize over the head of the sleeping subject. These projections are first seen originating from the forehead of the sleeping human; no changes in sleep mannerism have been recorded during this process. As a group, the SCP-1443-1 seek out a suitable location for rest, usually a patch of open soil. Should the SCP-1443-1 find a place of rest within fifteen minutes, they will cluster together and form a new individual of SCP-1443. If no such place is available, the SCP-1443-1 disappear when fifteen minutes have elapsed. Juvenile instances of SCP-1443 “planted” in this manner do not appear immediately capable of generating SCP-1443-1. The length of time required for these new plants to fully mature is currently unknown.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarities between this symbiotic relationship and that observed in SCP-667 have been noted. Investigation into a correlation of the two anomalies is ongoing.

item.title: Indeed.
item.content:encoded: εїз

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
spoooooooilers

The vines are a gateway from the Underworld to the world above. Sometimes souls are given another chance, if they've waited on the shores of the Styx enough and they didn't have the means to pay the boatman when they first landed there. The butterflies are the forms that they take because they effectively weren't judged yet, but they can still hang around and help other souls who couldn't make it all the way.

I don't think there'd be an origin restriction for the souls, though the majority of souls that do go through the process are most likely from people who believed in Hades and have been sitting on the Styx shores for some time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Spoilers
– hide block

So… the trees produce people's souls when they die? Like, they go to the Underworld, drink the water, and temporarily end up visiting the land of the living through the tree?And then, is it only people in Greece whose souls come through this thing?
This is making me really curious, in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Before I looked at the page history or the discussion, I saw mention of butterflies and thought "I suspect Zyn did this." It's pretty neato.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am going to have to look for that kind of story, because I love Greek mythology and I've never heard of that. All I know if that they place coins on the eyes of the deceased to pay for the ride from the ferryman Charon across the river Styx into Hades.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never knew about the soul butterflies thing despite reading heavily into Greek mythology (damn reference books didn't mention it at all) but I caught the reference to the river. Nice job, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Edited!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe "a specific arrangement of four leaves, which resembles the forewings and hindwings of Lepidoptera"? It's a bit longer (and means to reflect a slight additional change after the comma), but it does help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's a little weird to parse. Suggestions on improvement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta say, the phrase "pairs of two" bugs the shit out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My personal take was that they behave like normal, solid butterflies, but no one can get close enough to determine for sure if they're tangible. I guess the Foundation could attempt tests (firing bullets at the butterflies) but it's not seen as high on the priority list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Roughly every 24 hours, SCP-1443 will generate iridescent projections of butterflies

Projections? So, like holograms? Or are they solid butterflies that appear out of nowhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, I haven't laughed that hard in a while. I can't unmake the connection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Drewbear tagged most of it. I just fixed some inaccuracies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Lunesta can cause risk of suicide and potentially fatal throat swelling?! ._.

That said, luna moths are pretty…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My knowledge of ancient Greek myth is a bit patchy but I distinctly recall the dead transforming into butterflies when they enter the underworld and drink the water there (along with losing their living memories). The audio log allowed me to put 2 and 2 together, so I commend the creator for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first started reading this, I was 90% sure you were making an SCP out of this:

http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vu0rXFhsM8w

Man, that ad has always creeped me out every time I've seen it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you like it, Wilt. :3

Also, thanks for the help with the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It stays somewhat subtle while still making the connection, and while it's simple, it manages to remain interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've studied a lot of Greek mythology (does God of War count?) but I still didn't make the connection when I read this. However, it is an interesting and well-written idea even if it what it is wasn't immediately obvious to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't feel anything with this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was fairly meh on this until the last note. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only this one is a bit more than a compulsive, killer swarm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, but not quite like this. This is shorter, safer, simpler, and is quite a pleasant read. The connection to Greek mythology was particularly impressive. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An anomalous vine that manifests fairies? It's been done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, thanks! I'm glad you felt the addition of new content was worthwhile.
now please get some sleep sleep is good ;^; this is not worth sleep deprivation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Today. Not too long ago. I think.

;A;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, when did I give you feedback?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw this one on your sandbox and I liked it, but now you added something really intriguing and the result is that I'm so happy to read it (despite the severe sleep deprivation I'm suffering while writing this).

+1 (and +4 in my head, wich is 100% soul-butterflies free)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, the people I showed the article to found it rather bland with just the vector for seed spreading. Could be personal taste, though.

I'm sure it is. If they needed the extra stuff to find the article interesting, and I found the article interesting despite not liking that bit quite as much, then I think the article as it stands is striking the proper balance. Well, maybe I'd like it more if there were some ambiguity (so that in my personal headcanon I could pretend that the butterflies were psychic seeds that had nothing to do with the underworld except maybe as a historical inspiration for some myths), but the article as it stands is making people happy, so kudos.

Thanks for the comment

Thanks for the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
after that the references to the Classical underworld seemed tacked-on and less interesting

Actually, the people I showed the article to found it rather bland with just the vector for seed spreading. Could be personal taste, though.

moar spoilers?
hide

Also, the butterflies themselves are not exactly seeds. Perhaps check the collapsible in my first post?

If I were dealing with an out-of-control plant, I think I'd rather have a chainsaw or a gallon of gasoline on hand than a spray bottle of Roundup.

It was inside a Foundation greenhouse? Edit: Also, on the off chance that one of the butterflies popped up, I'm not sure you'd want to fall asleep holding a chainsaw.

Thanks for the comment, I really appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like the article without the clear association with the Greek underworld. A tree that uses a psychic vector to spread its "seeds" is unexpected and quirky enough for me to instantly love it, and after that the references to the Classical underworld seemed tacked-on and less interesting. It's possible that I might be missing something because of my merely passing familiarity with Greek mythology.

Also, a nitpicky question. Why did they attack the plant with herbicides? It seems like axes or fire would be a lot faster and more decisive. If I were dealing with an out-of-control plant, I think I'd rather have a chainsaw or a gallon of gasoline on hand than a spray bottle of Roundup. It didn't affect my appreciation of the article at all, it's just something that made me go "huh?"

I still liked it a lot, +1.

item.title: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to Drewbear, who gave me a key idea during the initial stages of the draft. Further thanks to Scantron, Djoric, DrMann, Jekeled, Eric_H (naming credit!), thedeadlymoose, MisterFlames, Rumetzen, and Silberescher who all gave me feedback and suggestions during the later stages.

Totally belated edit: Thanks to Scantron for photoshopping the original picture to make it look nicer. For the record, the picture was taken by me with my cell phone, at my university.

This took awhile to write, admittedly. Yes, there are references to Greek Mythology.

Potential spoilers
hide


Psyche = soul = Ancient Greek, "butterfly"

The river shore mentioned isn't one found on earth.

For those who may be interested… 1999, Athens, Greece, 143.
